column2: The lavender color, however, didn't smell of lavender, but stank of the thing that would keep various insects from gnawing at the terrace.
— You know, — said Miyechka, sniffing the paint, because she'd always loved its smell, — I wouldn't even mind if these boards were a little worm-eaten.
Do you remember the patterns we used to seek out on these boards when we were little?

It was a little sad to tear up the worm-eaten boards, on which were written somewhere the first letters of the names of first loves, on which she and her sister had secretly drawn little devils and ballerinas, and then said that they had appeared by themselves by some miracle.
Now the two of them sat on the step of the new terrace.
It smelled of paint and of a spicy summer evening.
The old boards were stacked on the grass.
They went over to them and began looking them over, searching for names.
And Miyechka eventually found on a board a long-forgotten inscription: 'M + Ya.'
— Who's that about?
— asked Miyechka.
— About Yaroslav.
Do you remember him?
The one I showed you on Facebook.
— So you didn't tell me anything back then?
— And when did I ever tell you anything at all?
At nine I was wiser and preferred to live through all my dramas alone, — said Lilichka.
— Oh, everyone knew about all your dramas anyway.
So what was this Yaroslav like?
— Oh, the one who's bald now, soft sort of, and with a son exactly like him.
And the wife is beautiful.
Just imagine, I could have given birth to that kid!
— Let's scratch something on the new terrace, — said Miyechka.
— What, now?
— Why not?
— With what?
— With these keys here.
They knelt down and found a board that wouldn't catch anyone's eye in the morning.
— What will you scratch?
— asked Lilichka.
— What do you think?
— Scratch 'M + U.'
Miyechka took the Honda keys out of her shorts pocket, clicked the button on the key and said:
— This is the only way for me to stay in his heart forever.
Night was descending onto the terrace.
A dim little light glowed in the window — it meant everyone was in the living room.
Perhaps Lusya was sleeping on the grandmother's chest, while Marta and Maria sat murmuring about something barely audibly.
Miyechka had already finished the 'M,' then diligently scratched out the '+' and stopped.
— Having doubts?
— Lilichka whispered.
She lay beside her on her stomach and watched intently as the letters emerged on the brand-new, even board.
— No, — retorted Miyechka.
— I'm thinking, maybe you know some love spell?
— A love spell or an un-love spell?
— What's the difference?
— Well, I don't exactly know…
Suddenly the front door creaked.
— Girls, — came the grandmother's voice, — what are you doing here?
— A cockchafer!!!
— Lilichka shouted.
— Right, a cockchafer in the middle of July, — Miyechka muttered through her teeth.
— Come inside, you'll freeze.
— Granny, it's not cold.
— I'm always cold.
After eighty the grandmother had begun, with renewed vigor, to insistently bundle everyone up.
— I'll lock the door now and you'll be left to sleep outside, — said the grandmother.

Both sisters froze, it was so pleasant to hear all this, as if they were little again, as if you really could be left to sleep outside, as if the grandmother's anger still frightened them even a little.
— Granny, keep going, I already feel twenty years younger, — said Lilichka, laughing.
— Me too, — said the grandmother and shut the door.
Miyechka rolled onto her back with the key in her hand and the unfinished letter 'U' in her thoughts.
Above the cottage the stars glimmered, by the river the willow branches rustled, barely audible.
— God, how good it is, — she said.
— Remember how good it is for us right now, — replied Lilichka.
She too lay on her back.
Miyechka rolled back onto her stomach.
The boards, still from the hot day, were slightly warm and smoothly sanded not only by tools, but by divine voices too.
She took the keys again and, biting her lower lip, scratched an 'L' after the plus.
Lilichka looked, and then took the key from her and drew a little heart around the whole inscription.
— I'm not going to Australia, — she said.
— What do you need those kangaroos for, — replied Miyechka.
— Better let's rent a van with a tent on the roof and go to that Australia, just the two of us.
— Or maybe it'd be better, then, to settle here together and, besides the ginger one, get twenty more cats?
— Let that be plan B.
— You know, I don't need any U at all if I can't even have a proper fight with him, — said Miyechka, holding the keys in her hand.
— Every time I fight with him, I do it only in my thoughts, because I know — he'll just disappear then.
He has an iron will, he's stronger than I am.
— I think there's no point even being friends with people you can't have a fight with, — said Lilichka.
— Shall we go to Australia?
— Of course we will!
— said Miyechka simply because at that moment it didn't suit her to answer anything but 'yes.'
Just as it hadn't suited her to say 'no' to Danylo that April day on the lovers' bridge, toward which she walked knowing what would happen there.
The next day, first thing in the morning, the terrace was evenly covered with peeling, aged lavender Provençal, and under the layer of paint the little heart 'M+L' was hidden away for decades.
The Recipe for Holubtsi
Marta had been feeling unwell since early morning.
The children, for some reason, now kicked, now went still.
Then she would get up and anxiously walk back and forth, trying to stir them up.
— Children aren't supposed to be moving inside you all the time, — said the grandmother.
— You won't believe it, but they're almost people in there.
They sleep!
Marta felt uneasy.
And the others felt ill at ease too.
Maria said they should go to the doctor.
Marta replied that there was no need.
— I just have my spells sometimes, — she looked straight ahead, calmly.
It seemed that if you took her pulse just then, her heart wouldn't be beating a single beat faster.
By no sign could you tell that Marta was worried.
She breathed evenly, just sometimes got up and walked.
But gathered around her now were those who knew her best.
Those who, by a movement of her hand, by a glance, by an extra touch to her hair or a few steps in a certain direction, could tell that Marta felt unwell.
She understood this and got angrier still because of it.
So everyone else pretended not to notice anything.
When Marta went into her room and lay down, staring palely at the ceiling, Maria knocked at her door.
She just came in and sat down at the table near the bed.
She put her elbows on the table and pressed her fists under her chin.
— It'll just be a shame if I ruin my waist and they don't survive, — said Marta, not looking at Maria.
— Let's go to the doctor, we'll see how they're doing in there.
— I'm not a hysteric.
— I know, you're just worried about your waist.
— I'd need to buy something for my bottom half, the only thing that fits me is this stupid polka-dot skirt.
— All the more reason.
We'll stop by the doctor's if you want.
Let's go for a skirt, — Maria started to get up from the table, then sat down again and said: — I've gotten a bit sick of this ivy.
I'd stay in the city.
— Let me stay with you.
They got themselves ready within an hour, without haste and without fuss — in this house only the two of them knew how to do that.
Lilichka offered to drive them, so as not to be left without the car.
Miyechka put her arm around the grandmother's shoulders, put on dark glasses and made faces after the departing car.
— If only they get there safely, — sighed the grandmother.
— Granny, it's an hour's drive for them.
— It's a road all the same.
If only they'd left the child.
They wanted to feed Lusya ice cream and cotton candy.
For that they'd dressed her in a gray full ballerina skirt and a white T-shirt with a little heart.
— Granny, and to the end of the village — is that a journey for you?
— No, that's within the village.
— And to the next one?
— Sort of.
— And beyond the next one?
— What are you fishing for?
— I want to find out how many kilometers count as a journey for you, and how many count as 'sort of.'
— Better to go on foot.
Miyechka and the grandmother sat outside for a long time, but suddenly the grandmother turned pale and said she was cold.
— Come, I'll run you a bath and bring you some champagne in there!
— said Miyechka.
— Run it, I'll warm up, you can skip the champagne.
I'm cold.
Miyechka went to the bathroom, dug out from all over the place the dregs of bubble bath scented with strawberry, currant and peach.
She mixed it all into the bath, the foam came out thick and fragrant.
Midday light sifted into the bathroom through the little window.
For atmosphere she also lit a candle and set it on the washing machine.
She poured some sparkling water and squeezed a few drops of lemon into it.
She left the glass on the edge of the bath.
The things thrown on the laundry basket she stuffed into the washing machine, screwed the caps back on the open tubes of creams, pastes and masks and arranged them on the shelf.
She went and put on a short black dress and even found a lacy little apron that had once reached her knees, and now covered only her belly.
She combed her hair.
— You could film me in a movie about a maid right now, — she said to herself, looking in the mirror and meekly tilting her head to one side.
She found her phone, photographed herself in character and sent it.
— Who are you playing with?
— she received in reply.
— It's not what you think, — she wrote and left the phone in the house.
The grandmother was already at the threshold.
— Madam's bath is ready, — said Miyechka, standing in the doorway with her hands folded on the apron.
The grandmother laughed, then saw the bath, the foam and the improvised champagne.
In the bath she warmed up at first.
She even lay there, looking at the ceiling, and then said:
— Miyechka, I feel unwell, — she began to shake, she grabbed the edge of the bath with her hands.
Miyechka helped her out of the foam.
The grandmother was light, like a teenager.
She threw a towel over her shoulders.
Now she couldn't control her body at all, it was shaking, her eyes were rolling back, she couldn't hold Miyechka's fingers in her hand.
— Miyechka, — she said.
Solomiya stood over the grandmother, holding her firmly by the shoulders.
Neither said anything, but she so wanted it not to be silent.
— Granny, — she said, — it'll pass, it'll pass in a moment.
The grandmother got no better at all.
It seemed to last an eternity.
— Don't worry, — said the grandmother.
— Granny, — Miyechka spoke up again, — tell me how you make holubtsi.
The grandmother gathered her strength and began:
— Take a small head of cabbage, — her words weren't clear as usual, but somehow squeezed out, blurred.
— Trim off the little veins.
— Aha, — Miyechka nodded.
— And the filling, the filling?
— Put the meat in in little pieces, — the grandmother breathed out.
— Boil the rice, so that it's already cold.
It seemed her words were becoming easier and coming out more freely.
Miyechka put her arm around her shoulders, and the grandmother spoke into her neck, having rested her head against Miyechka's chest.
— Don't be afraid of overcooking, it'll get overcooked anyway.
Under her hands it seemed to Miyechka that the grandmother's shoulders were no longer trembling so much, that her voice was becoming calmer.
About the spoonful of sugar and the sour cream in the sauce the grandmother spoke almost calmly already.
Miyechka wiped the foam off her shoulders and walked her to the bed.
Only afterward did she notice that her own dress was all wet, that her hands were trembling and she wanted to cry.
— But at least I got washed, — the grandmother spoke up from the bedroom.
— And I know how to make holubtsi.
— And will you make them?
— I will.
— So what, did you think I'd die and you were afraid of losing the holubtsi recipe?
— Of course.
Both of them knew that Miyechka had no use whatsoever for holubtsi.
She called Lilichka, and she arrived within half an hour.
